message.content:
The discrepancy you're noticing in the listing of the 10-year Treasury note yield (TNX) between Yahoo Finance and platforms like CBOE and Google Finance is due to how each platform chooses to represent the yield data.

1. **Yahoo Finance**: This platform typically lists the 10-year Treasury yield (TNX) by dividing the yield percentage by 10. So, if the actual yield is 3% (or 0.03 in decimal terms), Yahoo Finance would display it as 0.30.

2. **CBOE and Google Finance**: These platforms usually represent the yield directly as a percentage. So, the same 3% yield would be displayed as 30 on these platforms.

The way Yahoo Finance displays TNX can be confusing because they use a different unit—basis points instead of percentage points—without explicitly indicating this unit difference in the display. A basis point is equal to 0.01%, so what Yahoo does essentially converts the percentage into a format that looks like basis points for easier comparison across different interest rates, albeit without the explicit labeling.

When using these platforms, it's crucial to be aware of this difference to interpret the data accurately. Always check whether the figure you're looking at is in percentage terms or adjusted to basis points to avoid any misinterpretation.
